skill.name: react-observability
skill.description: Guidance for adding observable logging, diagnostic errors, flow tracing, and error reporting to React and React Native applications.
skill.summary: Improve React debugging with structured logs, useful errors, redaction, tracing, and production error capture.
skill.author: CJHarmath

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged many external command, environment access, key, and cryptography patterns in SKILL.md. Manual review found a documentation-only React observability guide, with code fences and TypeScript template literals causing most command and cryptography hits. The remaining concern is medium risk because examples encourage rich logging and production error reporting, which can expose sensitive data if copied without stronger safeguards.

medium_findings.title: Privacy-Sensitive Logging Guidance Requires Care
medium_findings.description: The skill recommends adding detailed context to logs, errors, state snapshots, and production error reports. It also includes redaction guidance, but users could still copy examples that log IDs, state, stack traces, and service context before applying data minimization.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The documentation clearly promotes context-rich logging and external error reporting. It also tells users to redact sensitive data, so this is an implementation caution rather than evidence of malicious intent.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Markdown and TypeScript Examples Flagged as Shell Execution
low_findings.description: The scanner treated Markdown code fences and TypeScript template literals as Ruby or shell backtick execution. The reviewed file is instructional Markdown and does not define executable scripts or commands.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged backticks are visible inside Markdown examples and TypeScript string templates. No evidence of runtime shell invocation or command construction was found in SKILL.md.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Environment Access Limited to NODE_ENV Examples
low_findings.description: The only environment access reviewed checks process.env.NODE_ENV to select log level or production error reporting behavior. No secrets are read, persisted, or transmitted by the skill itself.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Both occurrences reference NODE_ENV in sample React utility code. This is a common configuration check and not evidence of credential access.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Sensitive Keyword and Weak Cryptography Matches Are Textual
low_findings.description: The scanner flagged words such as key, secret, token, apiKey, and the markdown filename context. These appear in a redaction example and documentation text, not in code that handles certificates, keys, or cryptographic algorithms.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The sensitive terms are used to teach redaction and object-key iteration. No evidence of certificate file access, private key handling, hashing, or encryption logic was found.

dangerous_patterns.title: External Error Reporting Example
dangerous_patterns.description: The Sentry example sends captured errors and context to a production reporting service. This is legitimate observability behavior, but users must sanitize context before enabling it.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The example clearly calls Sentry.captureException with extra context. It is not malicious, but it can become risky if sensitive fields are included.

dangerous_patterns.title: Stack Trace and State Logging Examples
dangerous_patterns.description: Several examples log stack traces and selected application state. These patterns support debugging but can disclose internals or user data if used without redaction.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The examples explicitly include stack and state-like context in logs. The risk depends on implementation data, so confidence is high for the pattern and moderate for impact.

content.value_statement: React failures are hard to diagnose when logs and errors lack context. This skill gives practical patterns for structured logging, redaction, tracing, and production error capture.
actual_capabilities: Provides patterns for replacing silent returns with contextual warning logs.
Shows how to design error messages with diagnostic state and likely causes.
Defines structured logging levels and a simple logger wrapper for React code.
Includes redaction guidance for passwords, tokens, API keys, and similar fields.
Covers flow tracing, state snapshots, assertion helpers, and React error boundaries.
Demonstrates production error reporting with a Sentry-style capture function.
limitations: Does not install or configure a full observability platform.
Does not provide runnable package code or automated instrumentation.
Requires teams to adapt examples to their logging and privacy policies.
Redaction examples are basic and may not cover every sensitive data shape.

use_cases.title: Diagnose React Production Failures
use_cases.description: Add structured logs and useful errors around failing user flows so production incidents have clear evidence.

use_cases.title: Improve React Native Debugging
use_cases.description: Trace async flows and state transitions in mobile screens without relying on silent failures or vague errors.

use_cases.title: Standardize Team Logging Practices
use_cases.description: Create shared rules for log levels, redaction, error boundaries, and temporary debug logging.

prompt_templates.title: Add Basic Logging
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner request for one component or function.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the react-observability skill to review this React function. Add contextual logs for early returns and failures, and explain each change briefly.

prompt_templates.title: Improve Error Messages
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the react-observability skill to rewrite these React error messages. Include function location, failed condition, relevant state, and a likely cause where useful.

prompt_templates.title: Trace an Async Flow
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the react-observability skill to instrument this async React flow. Add a flow ID, step logs, state snapshots, and sanitized error reporting.

prompt_templates.title: Design an Observability Standard
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the react-observability skill to create a logging and error-handling standard for our React app. Include levels, redaction rules, error boundaries, and review checks.

output_examples.input: A React save handler returns early when an ID is invalid.
output_examples.output: The response adds a warning log before the return, includes the ID and safe counts, and explains why the branch matters.

output_examples.input: A checkout flow fails in production with only a generic error.
output_examples.output: The response proposes flow tracing with start, step, success, and failure logs, plus sanitized context for the reporting service.

output_examples.input: A team wants safer logs before enabling Sentry.
output_examples.output: The response lists redaction rules, fields to avoid, context allowlists, and checks for error boundary reporting.

best_practices: Log why code exits early, but keep user data minimized and sanitized.
Use structured context objects so logs are searchable and consistent.
Gate temporary debug logs and remove them before release.
anti_patterns: Do not log passwords, tokens, payment data, or full user records.
Do not swallow errors without logging or rethrowing when needed.
Do not send raw application state to external reporting tools.

faq.question: Does this skill work with React Native?
faq.answer: Yes. The guidance applies to React web and React Native, especially async flows, state debugging, and error capture.

faq.question: Does it require Sentry?
faq.answer: No. Sentry is only an example. You can adapt the patterns to any error reporting service.

faq.question: Can it write production logging code?
faq.answer: It can guide code changes and examples, but you must adapt them to your logger, privacy rules, and runtime.

faq.question: How does it reduce noisy logs?
faq.answer: It recommends log levels, contextual messages, and temporary debug gates so routine logs stay controlled.

faq.question: Does it handle sensitive data?
faq.answer: It includes basic redaction guidance. Teams should add allowlists and policy-specific checks before production use.

faq.question: Is this a security tool?
faq.answer: No. It improves observability for React debugging, but it also includes privacy cautions for logging sensitive data.
